text: The DaVinci Intuitive robotic system allows us to operate doing hernia surgery.
It's particularly useful, I think, in colorectal cancer.
We start just the same way as we would for any laparoscopic or open surgery.
The surgical team places the robotic arms and ports, known as trocars, under direct vision, preparing the surgical field for robotic-assisted surgery.
by using the camera and looking at the screens.
Once we place the ports where we need them to be for the surgery, I think then the robot becomes docked, i.e.
then we have it in the corner in the beginning, then we place it and connect it to the patient, and then we connect and choose the right instruments.

The surgeon scrubs out and moves to the robotic console, where they sit and look into a viewfinder. The console has two arms that hold the surgeon's fingers, allowing the robot to mimic the surgeon's finger movements inside the patient.
And I think it's very precise.
Not only can you see things magnified, but I can adjust the magnification.
I have certain dyes that are available to me that show me a good blood supply or structures in the body that you can't see just laparoscopically.
With the robot, we can have you stay overnight and go home the next day and really have minimal pain.
And I think that's really the beauty of it.
And you're looking at, you know, anywhere from three to six tiny incisions depending on what you're having done.
